title: High numerical aperture confocal volumetric mesoscope reveals mesoscale subcellular dynamics in vivo - Nature Biotechnology
text:
Download PDF

AbstractSimultaneously capturing subcellular dynamics across thousands of cells in mammals remains challenging because of trade-offs among spatial resolution, field of view, imaging speed and sample viability, particularly under high-NA (numerical aperture) conditions. Here we design a high-NA mesoscale objective lens and present a confocal volumetric mesoscope with an NA of 1.05, RUSH3D-HR, achieving ~390-nm resolution across a volume of 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3 at five volumes per second with low phototoxicity. By integrating our previously developed confocal scanning light-field framework with a parallelized deep-learning pipeline, RUSH3D-HR increases experimental efficiency by orders of magnitude. Long-term imaging at 70 billion voxels per second visualizes immune responses such as long-distance collective cell migrations during wound healing and large-scale migrasome generation and neutrophil–macrophage interactions during acute liver failure. Hour-long monitoring over 6,000 neutrophils reveals transient emergent swarming behaviors in mouse spleen during lipopolysaccharide-induced inflammation, which is suppressed with electroacupuncture at ST36, providing direct evidence for the immunomodulatory mechanism of peripheral electrical stimulation.

MainMillions of cells and organelles in mammals continuously migrate, interact and orchestrate to form sophisticated populational dynamics that drive diverse functionalities in different physiological or pathological states within live mammalian organs1,2,3,4,5, which are hard to reproduce in vitro6. Recent developments of mesoscale intravital fluorescence microscopy3,7,8,9,10 enable the study of large-scale intercellular interactions at single-cell resolution across a mammalian organ in their native states, promoting diverse biological applications11,12,13. However, the progression of many diseases14,15,16, especially at the early stage, involves intricate subcellular dynamics in addition to coordinated cellular responses emerging across entire organs17. Comprehensive understanding of these transient mesoscale immune dynamics for basic research18 and drug discovery19 requires high-fidelity high-throughput volumetric imaging at submicron resolution with millimeter-scale field of view (FOV) and low phototoxicity, which remains a systemic engineering challenge because of the requirement of high numerical aperture (NA)20,21.NA describes the light collection angle and is a fundamental parameter of a microscope, characterizing the light-gathering ability and spatial resolution set by the diffraction limit. There are three technical challenges restricting the development and application of a high-NA mesoscope to achieve both large FOV and high resolution. Firstly, high-NA imaging systems are more sensitive to optical aberrations originating from either imperfect lens design and fabrication or nonuniform distribution of the refractive index in tissue. Therefore, the FOVs of existing objective lenses with a full NA larger than 1.0 are generally smaller than 1 mm (ref. 22). While adaptive optics can correct optical aberrations, it is usually time consuming and requires complicated systems for multisite correction of spatially nonuniform aberrations23,24. Secondly, high NA leads to a shallow depth of field (DOF) and usually requires dense axial scanning to cover a large three-dimensional (3D) volume2,20, which may also introduce high phototoxicity because of unnecessary excitation of out-of-focus regions in confocal microscopy11,25. Single-objective oblique light-sheet techniques26,27 can achieve fast volumetric imaging with a reduction in phototoxicity; however, they remain incapable of providing millimeter-scale high-NA imaging at millisecond speeds. Lastly, the shallow DOF results in strong contaminations of background fluorescence in scattering tissue, degrading the imaging contrast and fidelity in vivo. Although recent developments in computational imaging shed light on solving these problems individually3,28,29,30,31,32,33,34,35, addressing them simultaneously to visualize organelle-level subcellular structures across mesoscale FOVs in the native state remains technically unachievable.Here, we present a confocal volumetric mesoscope with an NA of 1.05, named RUSH3D-HR, achieving subcellular resolution (about 390 nm laterally and 700 nm axially) across a large volume of 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3 at 5 VPS (volumes per second) with low phototoxicity. Unlike prior mesoscale systems that struggle with the high-NA aberration sensitivity, phototoxicity and background contaminations, RUSH3D-HR addresses these challenges through a synergistic integration of three core advances. Firstly, our designed high-NA mesoscale objective lens (named Kunlun) expands the NA to 1.05 over a 2.8-mm FOV to achieve a large lateral space–bandwidth product (SBP; Methods), implemented with wave-optics digital adaptive optics (wDAO)3,31 to computationally correct aberrations. Compared to existing mesoscale objective lenses of similar NA22, Kunlun achieves an improvement in lateral SBP of one order of magnitude; furthermore, when compared to objectives with similar lateral SBP7,36, Kunlun’s NA is roughly twofold higher (Fig. 1a). Secondly, we use the scanning light-field framework29 for parallel high-speed volumetric acquisition while minimizing phototoxicity. Thirdly, our line-confocal scanning light-field strategy33 provides robust optical sectioning and background rejection across an extended 40-μm axial coverage, effectively overcoming the shallow DOF bottleneck of high-NA optics, to ensure that the full potential of the objective lateral SBP is realized. To manage the huge data throughput (70 billion voxels per second), we developed a fully automated parallelized deep-learning pipeline incorporating denoising37, multisite aberration correction31, motion artifact reduction32 and 3D reconstruction34. Diverse applications are demonstrated to show RUSH3D-HR’s versatility and robustness. In mouse skin, we tracked collective long-distance cell migrations and morphological diversity during the wound healing process for several hours. In an acute liver failure model induced by acetaminophen (APAP), we observed subcellular neutrophil–macrophage interactions and identified a significant increase in migrasome generation and neutrophil motility, demonstrating efficient discovery of rare immune events in a single trial and greatly reducing the number of animals required for in vivo experiments. By monitoring over 6,000 neutrophils for hours in the mouse spleen during lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced inflammation, we discovered transient emergent swarming behaviors of neutrophils randomly distributed across the spleen. With this mesoscale biomarker of inflammation, we validated that electroacupuncture (EA) at ST36 effectively suppressed inflammation, providing direct evidence of peripheral immunomodulation. These results highlight the broad utility of RUSH3D-HR in uncovering mesoscale intercellular emergent dynamics during disease progression, with applications spanning cell biology, immunology and pathology.Fig. 1: Principle of RUSH3D-HR.Full size imagea, Benchmark of lateral SBP and NA of state-of-the-art mesoscale objective lenses, illustrating the missing gap of high-NA mesoscale systems. The lateral SBPs of objective lenses are calculated theoretically from previous works and the information provided by the manufacturer (Methods). Light-blue dots represent the objectives used with one-photon imaging, while the dark-blue dots represent the objectives designed for multiphoton imaging. b, The imaging capability of RUSH3D-HR covering different scales from organelle level to organ level simultaneously. c, Optical design of the Kunlun, consisting of ten lens elements to achieve both large lateral SBP and high NA with uniformly low optical aberrations across the FOV. d, Comparison of NA and FOV between Kunlun and objectives with similar lateral SBP or NA, including a commercial objective (Olympus XLPLN25XWMP2) and customized objectives (Mesolens, Quadroscope objective and Schmidt objective). e, Optical layout of the RUSH3D-HR system, with the Kunlun magnified. f, Integration of the Kunlun objective in the confocal scanning light-field imaging framework with a GPU-parallelized data-processing pipeline to obtain high-speed high-resolution mesoscale intravital 3D imaging with multisite aberration correction. Raw measurements after confocal background rejection sequentially undergo motion correction, pixel realignment, segmentation, aberration estimation and 3D reconstruction with multisite aberration correction, all implemented in a GPU-parallelized pipeline for accelerated and robust processing. g, Example images with and without motion correction, with comparisons of SSIM shown on the right. In box plots, the center line represents the median, the box limits represent the lower and upper quartiles and the whiskers represent 1.5× the interquartile range (n = 20 samples). P = 1.26 × 10−19. Asterisks represent significance levels of comparisons between images without and with motion correction, tested using a two-sided independent t-test. *P < 0.05, **P < 10−2, ***P < 10−3 and ****P < 10−4. h, Comparison of reconstruction speed and image quality across different methods. Scale bars, 10 mm (c), 200 μm (original views in f), 30 μm (enlarged views in f), 5 μm (g) and 100 μm (h).Source dataResultsPrinciple of RUSH3D-HRDespite recent advances in mesoscale imaging to observe large-scale cellular dynamics at the organ level3,9, there remains a gap for subcellular mesoscopes because of the engineering challenge of designing and manufacturing a high-NA wide-FOV objective and the accompanying problems such as background contaminations and shallow DOF (Supplementary Video 1, part I). Therefore, it is generally hard to link microscale organelle behaviors with tissue-level functions of large-scale cells, which is critical for in vivo study of heterogeneous intercellular interactions (Fig. 1b).To address this problem, we firstly designed the Kunlun objective lens using a nontelecentric, full-path optical optimization approach38 (Fig. 1c and Supplementary Fig. 1). Unlike traditional telecentric designs, aberration correction is distributed across both the objective and tube lens, enabling a streamlined ten-element assembly that achieves near-diffraction-limited performance in simulation with uniform aberration distribution across the FOV. The customized Kunlun attains an NA of 1.05 over a vast 2.8-mm-diameter FOV with a large lateral SBP, while maintaining high optical transfer functions (OTFs) and low aberrations across a broad spectral bandwidth from 440 nm to 700 nm and the full imaging range (Supplementary Figs. 2 and 3). The field curvature and geometric distortion are maintained below 2% with the Strehl ratios exceeding 0.8 throughout the imaging plane. Silicone oil (with a refractive index of 1.406) is adopted as the immersion medium instead of water to maintain stable long-term imaging and achieve better refractive index matching with biological tissues39 relative to standard immersion oil (with a refractive index of 1.518). Compared to existing mesoscale objective lenses, we achieve about a twofold improvement in NA and much more uniform imaging performance in the spatial domain7,22,36 (Fig. 1d and Supplementary Fig. 4).However, because of the intrinsic aberration sensitivity of high-NA optics, even minute fabrication errors lead to inevitable resolution and SNR degradation and the situation becomes worse for intravital imaging because of the nonuniform distribution of the refractive index in multicellular organisms (Supplementary Fig. 5). To address this spatially nonuniform aberration, we then used the scanning light-field framework to extract high-resolution four-dimensional spatial–angular information, which can record the imaging process up to the diffraction limit for digital manipulation of the light rays in postprocessing29. This computational imaging framework facilitated the integration of wDAO3,31 into RUSH3D-HR, enabling accurate estimation and correction of multisite aberrations across a large FOV without reducing the data acquisition speed and thereby tolerating inevitable manufacturing and environmental errors (Supplementary Fig. 6).While the large NA increases photon efficiency, background contamination also becomes more severe with the shallow DOF, degrading the imaging contrast within living organisms. To overcome this problem, we extended confocal scanning light-field microscopy33 into the mesoscale realm by implementing a uniform, biaxially elongated line-confocal illumination that is precisely synchronized with camera rolling readout (Supplementary Fig. 7). This configuration uses the rolling shutter as a mobile virtual slit to effectively reject background fluorescence without optical descanning. Therefore, RUSH3D-HR achieves high-speed volumetric imaging with robust optical sectioning across millimeter-scale volumes within a compact platform (Fig. 1e).Furthermore, mesoscale subcellular imaging inherently generates huge data throughput. The raw spatial–angular data captured by RUSH3D-HR must undergo a multistage computational reconstruction to yield high-resolution 3D volumes. To handle the escalating data scale and complexity, we developed a fully accelerated artificial intelligence (AI)-based processing pipeline specifically tailored for RUSH3D-HR (Fig. 1f and Supplementary Fig. 8). Firstly, raw images were partitioned into 35 sub-FOVs for parallelized processing, where a blockwise correction strategy effectively rectified image distortions and dead pixels to correct nontelecentric distribution of chief rays (Supplementary Fig. 9). Secondly, we developed an automatic algorithm to identify motion artifacts during scanning and implemented the virtual scanning network32 to correct these artifacts (Fig. 1g and Supplementary Fig. 10). Thirdly, a self-supervised denoising strategy37 based on the spatial–angular correlation can be used to improve the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) by about 18 dB in extremely-low-photon conditions (Supplementary Fig. 11). The denoising process is optional depending on different samples. Lastly, to reconstruct the huge data size (about 14,000 × 10,000 × 100 voxels per frame, about 20 GB), conventional processing pipelines40,41 even with deep learning may take a very long time. We further improved our previously proposed self-supervised learning-based reconstruction network (SeReNet)34 with multi-GPU parallel processing and multisite wDAO, over 1,000-fold faster than deconvolution algorithms, thereby enabling real-time gigavoxel-scale processing (Fig. 1h and Supplementary Fig. 12).System characterization and benchmarkWe then comprehensively evaluated the performance of RUSH3D-HR and benchmarked it against state-of-the-art methods through multiple experimental validations.Firstly, resolution analyses by fluorescence beads and Fourier ring correlation (FRC) of diverse sample structures confirmed that RUSH3D-HR achieves uniform near-diffraction-limited performance throughout the entire imaging volume. Fluorescent beads with a diameter of 200 nm were randomly embedded in the 3D agar and imaged by RUSH3D-HR. The full widths at half maximum (FWHMs) of the bead intensity profiles along both lateral and axial dimensions demonstrate uniform resolution of 475 ± 53 nm laterally and 930 ± 54 nm axially across the mesoscale FOV (Fig. 2a). The degradation of the resolution compared to the diffraction limit results from system aberrations caused by manufacturing errors (Fig. 2b). Aberration correction with wDAO effectively solved this problem and increased the resolution to about 381 ± 42 nm laterally and 707 ± 50 nm axially with an effective axial range of about 40 µm (Fig. 2c,d and Supplementary Fig. 13a–d). Notably, the system maintains isotropic lateral resolution, despite a minor degradation observed when moving away from the native focal plane. With multisite aberration correction, the experimental resolution is uniformly distributed across the entire FOV of 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3 with a small standard deviation of only 48 nm laterally and 95 nm axially (Fig. 2e). This performance was validated against commercial spinning-disk confocal microscopy with a ×30 (1.05 NA) objective lens on static biological tissues, yielding nearly identical results and establishing the reliability of our system (Supplementary Fig. 13e,f). Standardized targets such as the 1951 USAF test chart also confirmed that RUSH3D-HR achieves a spatial resolution of 387.5 nm, clearly visualizing the line pair of element 3 in group 11 with a linewidth of 193.8 nm (Supplementary Fig. 14). We further validated the experimental resolution by measuring FRC on plant stem tissue and neutrophils in mouse spleen. Uniform resolution (399 ± 13 nm laterally and 717 ± 20 nm axially) can be obtained across the entire FOV in vivo with an effective depth range of 40 µm, designed to match the common one-photon scattering penetration depth in mouse liver and spleen (Fig. 2f and Supplementary Fig. 15). These resolution characterizations ensure high-quality uniform imaging performance across the mesoscale FOV at a high imaging frame rate, which is challenging for traditional mesoscopes.Fig. 2: Characterization of RUSH3D-HR.Full size imagea, Two-directional maximum intensity projections (MIPs) of 200-nm-diameter fluorescence beads randomly distributed within a volume of 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3, imaged by RUSH3D-HR. b, Orthogonal MIPs of five representative beads from different spatial regions without wDAO. c, Left, results after aberration correction using wDAO, showing improved performance. Right, estimated wavefronts. d, Box plots of the lateral and axial resolutions of RUSH3D-HR, quantified from FWHMs of Gaussian-fitted bead intensity profiles. Dashed lines indicate the diffraction limit (n = 832 beads for results without wDAO and 1,226 beads for results with wDAO included for statistical analysis). e, Heat maps of spatial resolution uniformity across the mesoscale FOV, without and with wDAO. f, A 3D rendering and enlarged MIPs of autofluorescent stem tissue slice, acquired by RUSH3D-HR. In box plots, the center line represents the median, the box limits represent the lower and upper quartiles and the whiskers represent 1.5× the interquartile range.Source dataSecondly, we evaluated the signal-to-background ratio (SBR) of RUSH3D-HR with the confocal module, which is critical for high-fidelity imaging in living tissues exhibiting intense background fluorescence. We imaged the superficial layer of a mouse liver with antibody labeling of the neutrophils and vessels in vivo with huge amounts of background fluorescence because of dense labeling. With the confocal scanning light-field framework33, RUSH3D-HR achieved a high SBR of 19.5 ± 0.8 dB, representing a substantial improvement of about 13 dB compared to its nonconfocal counterpart and about 15 dB relative to traditional wide-field microscopy (Fig. 2g, Supplementary Fig. 16 and Supplementary Video 1, part II). This enhanced optical sectioning enabled clear visualization of fine, subcellular structures such as macrophage filopodia within thick tissues and better image contrast across the entire FOV, thereby ensuring high-fidelity imaging in vivo.While traditional confocal microscopy can also achieve high-fidelity imaging, there is phototoxicity influencing the native cellular behaviors because of the unnecessary excitation out of the shallow DOF (<0.8 µm). RUSH3D-HR uses the light-field imaging framework to achieve a high-resolution extended DOF of about 40 µm, 50 times larger than traditional confocal microscopy with similar NA, which can solve this problem by keeping the photons focused within axially extended point spread functions (PSFs). We previously validated that the confocal scanning light-field imaging framework could intrinsically reduce the phototoxicity by more than two orders of magnitude compared to conventional confocal microscopy33. This advantage persisted at the millimeter-scale FOV in RUSH3D-HR, enabling long-term high-speed mesoscale subcellular imaging of neutrophils labeled using only standard antibodies, which are generally considered prone to rapid photobleaching (Fig. 2h). Both high SNR and clear structural visualization could be achieved with laser intensities as low as ~1 mW mm−2. Notably, there is no clear photobleaching in continuous 3D intravital imaging for over 3 h (>30,000 time points with millisecond-scale volumetric imaging speed), yielding more than 450 trillion voxels (Fig. 2i).Together, all these characterizations validate that RUSH3D-HR provides high-fidelity confocal mesoscale imaging with a uniform subcellular resolution of about 390 nm laterally and 700 nm axially over the entire FOV of 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3 at 5 VPS, delivering an effective data throughput of 70 billion voxels per second with minimal phototoxicity during prolonged imaging sessions. The performance is generally robust against strong optical aberrations, intense background fluorescence, sample motions and high noise levels.Monitoring of collective long-distance cell migrations during wound healingMesoscale subcellular intravital imaging is essential to observe collective cellular behaviors across a large range in animals such as immune responses and tumor metastasis. During skin wound healing, immune cells migrate directionally toward injury sites to regulate tissue repair in mice, requiring direct tracking of different types of cells within the native tissue microenvironments to understand its collective working mechanisms42,43. However, this process involves hours-long, long-distance migration and diverse cell morphological changes that conventional microscopy has struggled to capture in mammals34. RUSH3D-HR fills this gap.To demonstrate its capability, we continuously imaged innate immune responses in the dorsal skin of mice for over 10 h with automatic focus following a minor dermal injury induced by partial epidermal removal (Fig. 3a). Within its millimeter-scale 3D FOV, RUSH3D-HR simultaneously monitored collective cell behaviors at the wound site and surrounding tissues with subcellular details (Fig. 3b). Trajectory analysis of thousands of cells revealed collective long-distance cell migrations toward the wound during healing (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Video 2). Beyond this directed movement, we found that neutrophils navigated through the stromal compartment of dermal adipose tissue, forming net-like migration patterns (Fig. 3d). Notably, some neutrophils halted and gathered before reaching the wound, likely reflecting local sporadic inflammatory responses that recruited small immune clusters (Fig. 3e,f). Subcellular resolution of RUSH3D-HR enabled visualization of diverse cell poses among large neutrophil populations during disease progression. Such fine-grained subcellular information provides critical insights into how distinct cell shapes and pose transitions relate to migratory behaviors and functional states, underscoring the utility of RUSH3D-HR for studying mesoscale collective cell migrations in complex tissue contexts (Fig. 3g).Fig. 3: Long-term mesoscale tracking of long-distance collective neutrophil migrations during wound healing in mouse dorsal skin.Full size imagea, Schematic of the epidermal acute imaging window (diameter = 4.0 mm) in mouse dorsal skin, followed by induction of a dermal wound (diameter = 0.8 mm). Epi, epidermis; Dm, dermis; Sc, subcutis. b, MIPs and 3D renderings of wounded mouse dorsal skin labeled with neutrophils (magenta) and vessels (cyan) over a volume of 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3 acquired by RUSH3D-HR at three time points. Scale bars, 200 μm (b,c) and 20 μm (d–g).Source dataInvestigation of organelle-associated rare patterns in pathological statesThe dynamics of cells and organelles within mammalian organisms are highly sophisticated, encompassing common processes such as cell division, interaction and migration, as well as rare but functionally critical events for maintaining microenvironmental homeostasis44. For instance, the migrasome45, a newly discovered organelle that marks the path of migrating cells, has essential roles in developmental system formation46 and immune surveillance functions47, especially in pathological states. However, only a small subset of motile cells generate retraction fibers during migration, which may subsequently give rise to migrasomes, making these events inherently sparse. Observing their formation process and dynamic behaviors in vivo is challenging with conventional intravital microscopy, because of the inherent trade-off between FOV and resolution. Mesoscale subcellular imaging is crucial to capture the spatially extended interactions mediated by migrasomes, as well as to resolve organelle-level dynamics that underpin intercellular communication. Without such capabilities, studies often require dozens of experimental repetitions, posing obstacles for biologists studying the organelle-associated rare pathological patterns.To demonstrate the capability of RUSH3D-HR in uncovering organelle-associated rare patterns in mesoscale disease environments, we used three-color imaging of RUSH3D-HR to continuously observe neutrophils and macrophages in vessels of living mouse livers at 5 VPS (1.67 VPS for each channel). The mice were challenged with APAP for 4 h before imaging to establish acute liver failure. RUSH3D-HR effectively resolved clear subcellular morphologies across millimeter-scale FOV, corresponding to simultaneous imaging of hundreds of traditional microscopes (Fig. 4a and Supplementary Video 3, part I). Various subcellular rare events, such as large-scale migrasome generation from retraction fibers, which occur randomly within the spatiotemporal domain, became routinely observable during RUSH3D-HR imaging (Fig. 4b). Thus, RUSH3D-HR substantially enhanced the detection efficiency of rare events at the single-trial level, which can greatly reduce the number of animals required for in vivo experiments (Fig. 4c). With these large-scale data, we proceeded to investigate the role of migrasomes in APAP-induced liver failure. Compared to wild-type (WT) mice, APAP-challenged mice exhibited a significantly higher propensity to generate migrasomes with much higher neutrophil motion velocities, potentially as a means to establish immune surveillance (Fig. 4d,e and Supplementary Video 3, part II). Scale bars, 200 μm (whole views in a,e), 100 μm (first-level enlarged view in a), 10 μm (second-level enlarged view in a, enlarged views in b,h,i) and 5 μm (third-level enlarged views in a).Source dataMigrasomes have previously been reported to potentially act as intercellular messengers between immune cells29. Using RUSH3D-HR, we captured dynamic interactions between neutrophils and macrophages and discovered that a subset of migrasomes produced by neutrophils could be engulfed either by neutrophils themselves or by macrophages (Fig. 4h,i and Supplementary Video 4, part III). Notably, in APAP-challenged mice, these intercellular communications were substantially enhanced, accompanied by a significant increase in migrasome motility, from 0.13 μm s−1 to 0.29 μm s−1 (Fig. 4j). Moreover, >70% of migrasomes exhibited visible postformation energy enrichment under APAP challenge, in sharp contrast to <20% observed in controls (Fig. 4k). These observations suggest that, in the context of liver failure, migrasome activity is markedly upregulated, potentially functioning as a pivotal mediator of intercellular communications. By accelerating motility and enhancing postformation energy enrichment, migrasomes may facilitate immune cell coordination, thereby constituting a fundamental mechanism that underlies the orchestration of immune responses under pathological stress.Transient emergent neutrophil swarming during inflammation in mouse spleensEarly stages of disease progression in living organisms originate from subtle cellular changes within organs, where intricate subcellular dynamics coordinate with large-scale population-level behaviors. In particular, neutrophils can exhibit swarming, a collective, transient aggregation in response to inflammatory cues that amplifies immune efficacy while limiting tissue damage1,6. Each swarm represents a coordinated migration of hundreds of neutrophils into a dense cluster before gradually dispersing. Such emergent behaviors are essential for rapid immune responses, yet they have primarily been characterized ex vivo in cultured dishes and could not be observed within the mammalian spleen, where the densely packed and highly dynamic immune microenvironment has remained inaccessible because of technical limitations in FOV, resolution, phototoxicity and optical sectioning.Using RUSH3D-HR, we were able to directly capture this phenomenon in vivo. In LPS-challenged mouse spleens, imaging was initiated 3 h after injection to capture peak inflammatory responses (Fig. 5a and Supplementary Video 4, part I). Across a 2.8-mm-wide region, RUSH3D-HR revealed coordinated, population-level cell migration, leading to transient emergent swarming behaviors of neutrophils distributed randomly across the whole FOV (Fig. 5b and Supplementary Video 4, part II). Quantitatively, we tracked over 6,000 neutrophils per mouse for 5 h within the inflamed spleens. The number of neutrophils increased by approximately 30% compared to controls (Fig. 5c). Concurrently, their average migration velocity rose from 3.53 μm s−1 to 3.80 μm s−1 (Fig. 5d). These coordinated changes drove a nearly tenfold rise in swarming frequency, from about two events in controls to about 20 distinct swarms under LPS-induced inflammation (Fig. 5e,f). Such system-level responses that were previously inaccessible by the trade-off between FOV and resolution are clearly resolved by RUSH3D-HR, enabling detailed visualization of dynamic immune patterns of large-scale cells across both spatial and temporal scales throughout the entire organ during LPS-induced inflammation (Fig. 5g,h).Fig. 5: Large-scale transient swarming behaviors of neutrophils during inflammation in mouse spleens.Full size imagea, Schematic of the LPS-induced inflammation model. Mice received an intraperitoneal injection of LPS (5 mg kg−1) for 3 h to induce a proinflammatory phenotype. b, 3D renderings of an inflamed spleen labeled with neutrophils at 3 h after LPS challenge across 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3 and imaged with RUSH3D-HR. c,d, Box plot exhibiting cell number (c) and average velocity (d) for mice with PBS and LPS (n = 1,050 time frames). Unlike conventional biomarkers that often rely on endpoint assays or indirect molecular readouts, RUSH3D-HR enables real-time, high-throughput monitoring of mesoscale subcellular activities across spatiotemporal dimensions. The transient and collective nature of neutrophil swarming facilitates early detection of inflammatory states, while subcellular resolvability ensures accurate assessment of immune cell morphology and dynamics.To demonstrate this utility, we applied RUSH3D-HR to assess the anti-inflammatory efficacy of EA. This in vivo biomarker responds within hours rather than days and allows direct visualization of collective subcellular dynamics during early disease progression. RUSH3D-HR provides a high-fidelity panoramic view in the spatiotemporal domain, addressing the unmet niche of 3D intravital observation of large-scale intercellular interactions at subcellular resolution and millisecond-scale 3D imaging speed in mammals with minimal phototoxicity. Such a capability is enabled by the synergistic design of a high-NA wide-FOV objective lens and the confocal scanning light-field imaging framework in mesoscale, along with an end-to-end fully automated deep-learning processing pipeline to handle massive amounts of data in parallel. With the demonstration of RUSH3D-HR in diverse disease models, including wound healing, liver failure and spleen inflammation, the high-NA confocal volumetric mesoscope not only reveals previously inaccessible mesoscale subcellular behaviors with quantitative characterization of their spatiotemporal dynamics but also increases experimental efficiency by orders of magnitude, thereby greatly improving the reproducibility of intravital studies in biology.RUSH3D-HR differs from our previously developed RUSH3D3 by achieving much higher resolution through integrated technical advances and a customized objective lens. Optical design is inherently a process of trade-offs between resolution and FOV. To achieve 3D subcellular resolution (improving from 2.6 × 2.6 × 6 μm3 to 0.39 × 0.39 × 0.7 μm3), we engineered the Kunlun objective lens with a high NA of 1.05 and magnification of ×20. This advancement inevitably narrows the FOV from 8 × 6 × 0.4 mm3 to 2.7 × 2.0 × 0.04 mm3. For constructing the RUSH3D-HR imaging system, we customized a microlens array (MLA), where each individual pitch corresponds to 15 × 15 camera pixels, ensuring sufficient angular resolution for volumetric reconstruction, coordinated with high-speed scanning through a piezoelectric tilt platform to compensate for potential resolution loss caused by light-field modulation, allowing the system to approach the optical diffraction limit53 (Methods). Given their distinct spatial scales, RUSH3D-HR serves as a vital complement to RUSH3D for exploring the mesoscale subcellular microenvironment. While RUSH3D focuses on cellular resolution for population-level responses including cortical neuronal activity or germinal center formation, RUSH3D-HR enables the observation of organelle-level dynamics, such as the behavior of migrasomes or mitochondria in inflammatory environments. This capability provides a direct window into how organelle-driven changes in cellular states regulate the microenvironment to uncover fundamental biological mechanisms.There are still several limitations of RUSH3D-HR at the current stage, which can be addressed in the future by incorporating other techniques. Firstly, there is still a trade-off between snapshot effective axial coverage and resolution. Axial scanning with a large interval can be used for a large axial coverage of over 300 μm to cover dozens of layers of cells in cleared tissues at high speed (Supplementary Fig. 17). Wavefront coding methods can also be integrated to increase the effective depth range54. Secondly, the penetration depth is fundamentally limited by the tissue scattering and the one-photon excitation, especially in scenarios involving deeper imaging or highly scattering samples. Future improvements including the use of near-infrared fluorescence labeling techniques55 or the imaging framework of two-photon light-field excitation30 hold the potential to extend RUSH3D-HR’s capabilities to substantially deeper tissue layers. Thirdly, structured illumination approaches56,57 can be further incorporated into the current imaging system to achieve super-resolution mesoscale intravital imaging with better resolution for the detailed structures of organelles such as mitochondria and the endoplasmic reticulum during disease progression. Lastly, fluorescence labeling is required for molecular specificity, with only four spectral channels currently available for multiplexed imaging. Hyperspectral imaging or other imaging dimensions such as fluorescence lifetime can be used for simultaneous observation of more channels. In addition, conducting spatial transcriptomics58,59 analysis after functional imaging of RUSH3D-HR can provide additional molecular information to couple functional cellular behaviors, thereby delivering a more comprehensive landscape of mesoscale microenvironments during diverse physiological and pathological states.Nevertheless, RUSH3D-HR provides a subcellular mesoscale imaging platform to link microscale subcellular dynamics within each single cell and macroscale organ-level functions for the study of intercellular interactions in vivo.MethodsExperimental setupThe Kunlun was designed in a nontelecentric configuration, forming an imaging system in conjunction with the tube lens. The full optical assembly consists of 17 lenses, with a distance of over 90 mm between the objective and tube lens, providing sufficient space for the placement of a dichroic mirror. The surface of the objective lens in contact with the sample was designed to be flat for ease of silicone oil immersion. The optical and mechanical components were fabricated by Hangzhou Zhida Optics.In the construction of the RUSH3D-HR system, a multichannel laser source (LDI-primer-488, 89 North) was used for illumination, which has a built-in ‘despeckler’ function to avoid illumination speckles within the sample. The core of the confocal scanning light-field framework involves restricting the illumination beam to a light sheet and precisely synchronizing it with the camera rolling shutter of equivalent width. This mechanism achieves volumetric confocal imaging while effectively rejecting out-of-focus light beyond the 40-μm DOF. To achieve volumetric excitation, the linewidth of the light sheet along the y axis in our system is not minimized; instead, it is designed to be much larger than the optical diffraction limit. To implement this principle, the laser was passed through a cylindrical lens (focal length: 38 mm; LJ1765L1-A, Thorlabs), an optical slit (length: 5 mm, width: 200 μm) and several relay lenses before entering the objective lens, which project biaxially elongated line-confocal illumination onto the sample. The linewidth is approximately 16 μm along the y axis (Supplementary Fig. 7). Because of the low NA of the illumination beam, the variation in thickness across the 40-μm effective axial (z axis) range is negligible, ensuring consistent performance for 3D volumetric imaging. A one-dimensional (1D) galvanometer mirror was inserted into the illumination path to scan the light, covering the full FOV. On the detection path, the emitted fluorescence was reflected by a multiband dichroic mirror (ZT405/488/555/640, 50 × 70 × 5 mm; Chroma) and into a custom-built tube lens, before being captured by a high-resolution camera (QHY411, QHYCCD, pixel size of 3.76 μm). A customized MLA (square grid, pitch size of 56.4 μm, corresponding to 15 × 15 pixels, focal length: 536.4 μm, F-number: 9.5) was mounted on the sensor surface to achieve light-field modulation. To maintain a compact system, a two-dimensional (2D) piezoelectric tilt platform (P54.T0S, Core Morrow) was used to adjust the angle of the dichroic mirror, enhancing the spatial sampling rate. A 3D motorized stage (H117P1XD, PS3H122R, Prior Scientific) controlled the lateral and axial positioning of the sample holders. Customized control boards (BK-16-1005, Zhongke Zhenghong) were used to synchronize the camera, lasers, 2D tilt platform and 1D galvanometer for precise imaging operations. Detailed imaging conditions and parameters used in biological experiments, including fluorescent label, laser, excitation power, exposure time and volumetric imaging rate, are illustrated in Supplementary Table 1.Full-path optical optimization approach for KunlunFirstly, on the basis of the design requirements of NA of 1.05, magnification of ×20, large FOV and broad spectral bandwidth, we established an initial structure in Zemax OpticStudio software (version 19.4). To alleviate the design constraints on the objective lens, a finite conjugate configuration was adopted. The optical power was initially distributed between the objective and the tube lens, with lens curvatures and air gaps adjusted to ensure beam clearance and convergence at the image plane.Secondly, the objective lens was primarily optimized to correct spherical aberration, coma and chromatic aberration. In the early stages, a default merit function targeting the root-mean-square (r.m.s.) spot radius was used. By selecting appropriate glass materials and refining the radii of curvature, center thicknesses and air gaps, we systematically reduced the systemic chromatic and spherical aberrations, while the tube lens was used to balance the residual spherical aberration.Thirdly, once the primary aberrations were suppressed, the merit function was switched to minimize the optical path difference. We further controlled field curvature and geometric distortion by adjusting the curvature, spacing and materials of the rear lens groups within the tube lens assembly.Fourthly, during the fine-tuning phase, we focused on enhancing the OTF. Specific operands were added to the merit function to drive the OTF toward the diffraction limit across the entire FOV.Fifthly, we performed a manufacturability analysis for each element, incorporating constraints such as edge thickness and centering coefficients. A comprehensive tolerance analysis and sensitivity assessment were conducted using Monte Carlo simulations.For highly sensitive elements, we returned to the earlier optimization stages to redistribute optical power or adjust radii of curvature to improve stability. This iterative process yielded the final full-path optical optimization.AI-based automated processing pipelineTo fully leverage the advantages of RUSH3D-HR, an engineering-oriented integration was performed to enhance automation, robustness and efficiency, with the development of an AI-based processing pipeline. This pipeline is illustrated in detail in Fig. 1f and Supplementary Fig. 8. The individual components of the pipeline are described in detail.Autofocusing and levelling. Before data acquisition, the offset between two angular images in preview can be determined by calculating the spatial–angular correlation. When the sample is in focus, the overall image offset should be zero. A feedback mechanism is used to control the motorized stage, enabling autofocusing by raising or lowering the sample. Simultaneously, when the sample plane is not perpendicular to the optical axis, the offset along the 35 sub-FOVs will differ. Another feedback mechanism adjusts the three angular degrees of freedom of the motorized stage to achieve automatic levelling.Image acquisition and partition. The acquisition process of camera is synchronized with the multichannel lasers, 1D galvanometer mirror and 2D piezoelectric tilt platform. Each captured image is automatically segmented into 35 sub-FOVs for storage. Each sub-FOV contains 161 × 161 pixels and adjacent sub-FOVs overlap by 30 × 30 pixels to facilitate subsequent image stitching. The image acquisition speed can reach up to 6 fps.Distortion correction. Because of inaccuracies in the MLA fabrication or nontelecentric optical paths, spatially nonuniform distortion may occur in the raw light-field images. The distortion can be modeled with parameters and corrected through inverse mapping. The distortion parameters have two components, the intrinsic parameter matrix K, which defines the internal characteristics, and the distortion coefficients (k1, k2, k3, p1, p2), which model radial and tangential distortions. The intrinsic parameters matrix K is given by \(K=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{f}_{x} & 0 & {c}_{x}\\ 0 & {f}_{y} & {c}_{y}\\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\), where fx and fy are the focal lengths and (cx, cy) is the principal point. Given a normalized pixel coordinate (x, y, 1)T on raw light-field images, the intrinsic-parameter-corrected coordinate (u, v) is obtained by \(\left[\begin{array}{c}u\\ v\\ 1\end{array}\right]=K\left[\begin{array}{c}x\\ y\\ 1\end{array}\right]\). Then, the final undistorted coordinate (xd, yd) is computed by using distortion coefficients as$$\begin{array}{l}{r}^{2}={u}^{2}+{v}^{2}\\{x}_{d}=u(1+{k}_{1}{r}^{2}+{k}_{2}{r}^{4}+{k}_{3}{r}^{6})+2{p}_{1}uv+{p}_{2}({r}^{2}+2{u}^{2})\\ {y}_{d}=v(1+{k}_{1}{r}^{2}+{k}_{2}{r}^{4}+{k}_{3}{r}^{6})+{p}_{1}({r}^{2}+2{v}^{2})+2{p}_{2}uv\end{array}$$By manually annotating 12 coordinates at different positions across the entire FOV, the distortion parameters can be fitted with the least-squares method. Lastly, all the mappings from raw image coordinates to undistorted coordinates are calculated and segmented into 35 sub-FOV undistorted maps for parallel processing. The detailed pipeline for distortion correction is illustrated in Supplementary Fig. 9a. This process is system dependent and only needs to be calibrated once after constructing the system.Pixel realignment. In a light-field setup, each pixel behind a microlens corresponds to a specific angle53. Different microlenses represent different spatial sampling points. Through pixel realignment, light-field images can be transformed into spatial–angular images. In RUSH3D-HR, a 3 × 3 scanning mode is used to increase the spatial sampling rate, where the image shift distance is one third the size of each microlens. Spatial–angular images from nine scanning positions can be combined to form a high-resolution spatial–angular image.White balance. Because of nonuniform transmittance caused by the MLA defects or contamination, spatial–angular images may contain dead pixels that hinder the clear visualization of structures. To address this, a white balance operator is applied, as detailed in Supplementary Fig. 9b. A fluorescence plate is imaged and normalized to generate a standard matrix, which is then used to divide the captured biological images, removing dead pixels. This process is system dependent and only needs to be calibrated once after constructing the system.Motion correction. When the sample moves rapidly, noticeable changes occur across nine consecutive scanning light-field images, leading to motion artifacts in the spatial–angular images. To address this, we previously developed a virtual scanning network32 for motion artifact removal through deep learning. The virtual scanning network is a physics-based deep-learning framework that increases the spatial sampling rate of unscanned light-field measurements by exploring the frequency aliasing between different angles with broad generalization capability. However, this process relied on manually determining whether motion correction was necessary. To address this, we incorporated an automated module into the pipeline, as detailed in Supplementary Fig. 10. Because of the 3 × 3 scanning mode used in RUSH3D-HR, we introduced a Laplacian kernel60 \({K}_{m}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}{k}_{m} & {k}_{m} & {k}_{m}\\ {k}_{m} & {k}_{m} & {k}_{m}\\ {k}_{m} & {k}_{m} & {k}_{m}\end{array}\right),{\rm{where}}\,{k}_{m}=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}-1/8 & -1/8 & -1/8\\ -1/8 & 1 & -1/8\\ -1/8 & -1/8 & -1/8\end{array}\right)\), capable of effectively extracting regions with motion artifacts. The grayscale values in these regions are then transformed nonlinearly to create a weight map that reflects the motion level. A larger artifact results in a greater weight. To preserve the regions without motions as much as possible, the raw spatial–angular images and the results from the virtual scanning network are combined on the basis of the weight map. Typically, motion artifacts arising from local cellular dynamics are effectively and precisely rectified by our processing framework. Because the time interval for a single light-field acquisition is sufficiently short, the biological sample can be considered quasistatic during each individual capture. This ultrashort exposure time ensures that the sample is effectively fixed within each frame. Only a few subsets of moving cells exhibiting detectable artifacts require correction by the pipeline. Furthermore, for global movement of the tissue potentially induced by respiration, which manifests as consistent motion artifacts across all 35 sub-FOVs, the Laplacian kernel can also detect the presence of motion artifacts at every individual pixel, ensuring that the entire FOV is systematically processed and rectified by the virtual scanning network. We can also apply the exact same processing pipeline across all 35 sub-FOVs to ensure globally uniform correction. Consequently, global motion can be effectively rectified. The code for the virtual scanning network can be obtained from GitHub (https://github.com/THU-IBCS/VsLFM-master). During network training, we used simulated beads, bubbles, tubes and scanning volumes of sliced samples as the dataset and trained for 40 epochs, taking 1.5 h on an NVIDIA A100 GPU. It should be noted that this motion correction method is theoretically superior to TW-Net34, which is a more complex approach relying on prior self-supervised training to detect motion artifacts, followed by time-weighted smoothing correction. When the time-weighted coefficient is less than 1, it can lead to a loss of resolution and potential residual artifacts.Denoising. When the noise in acquired images is notable, it can adversely affect subsequent 3D reconstruction, leading to artifacts. To address this, we developed light-field denoising (LF-denoising37), a self-supervised transformer framework leveraging spatial–angular redundancy on the basis of the high-dimensional light-field measurements to achieve high-fidelity denoising without relying on temporal information and avoiding associated artifacts in the spatial domain. By treating multiple spatial–angular images as a collective whole, LF-denoising effectively suppresses noise without oversmoothing, thereby preserving high spatial resolution. However, the process still relied on manual determination of whether denoising was necessary. To address this, we incorporated an automated module into the pipeline, as detailed in Supplementary Fig. 11. The central angular view is used to calculate the single-image SNR (Methods). If the SNR exceeds 15 dB, the image is considered to require no denoising, whereas, if the SNR is less than 15 dB, the raw spatial–angular images are input into LF-denoising for reducing noise. The threshold is empirically determined. The code for LF-denoising can be obtained from GitHub (https://github.com/LF-denoising/LF-denoising). During network training, we used simulated beads, bubbles, tubes and scanning volumes of sliced samples as the dataset and trained for 50 epochs, taking 8 h on an NVIDIA A100 GPU. For inference, the spatial–angular data of each sub-FOV was divided into 25 patches, each with a size of 81 × 128 × 128 (view × height × width) for separate processing. The overlap region between adjacent patches contained 16 × 16 (height × width) pixels. Lastly, image stitching within each sub-FOV was performed on the basis of sigmoid fusion61.Aberration correction. Although optical design minimizes aberrations as much as possible, manufacturing errors and alignment eccentricities may still introduce slight, spatially nonuniform aberrations that remain in the optical system. Aberrations are difficult to remove in traditional 2D imaging31. To further correct system aberrations in RUSH3D-HR, wDAO3 is used to estimate spatially nonuniform aberrations through wave-optics modeling and generate corresponding PSFs for use in the subsequent 3D reconstruction process. By calculating the disparity distances between images from different angles, wDAO can dynamically correct the wavefronts across the entire FOV in postprocessing with the measurement of high-resolution light-field information, which estimates and compensates for the wavefront aberrations for every local region without reducing the data acquisition speed. In previous studies3, the full FOV was divided into 12 sub-FOVs for aberration estimation. In this work, we further subdivided the full FOV into 35 sub-FOVs as described above, resulting in an individual area of approximately 400 × 400 µm2, within which the aberrations can be considered relatively consistent, allowing wDAO to more finely correct spatially nonuniform aberrations. Note that a smaller sub-FOV can theoretically provide a more precise correction of spatially varying aberrations. However, because the marginal utility of finer partitioning is limited, the resulting enhancement in image quality would be minimal compared to the substantial escalation in computational cost. Therefore, dividing the entire FOV into 35 sub-FOVs is standardized throughout the pipeline.Parallel AI reconstruction and volume stitching. Because of the ultralarge volume of RUSH3D-HR data, each frame has a size of 14,000 × 10,000 × 100 voxels after reconstruction, nearing 20 GB. To address this, we used the previously developed SeReNet34 to accelerate the 3D reconstruction process. SeReNet is a physics-driven self-supervised network designed for the 3D reconstruction of light-field microscopy data, achieving near-diffraction-limited resolution with millisecond-level processing speeds. Its design is intended to eliminate the dependency on data-driven supervised training mechanisms, thereby facilitating robust performance across diverse biological samples. The code for SeReNet can be obtained from GitHub (https://github.com/kimchange/SeReNet). On a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, the reconstruction of single-frame data takes 139 s. To further speed up the process, we used eight GPUs in parallel, implementing parallelized SeReNet and reducing the reconstruction time for one frame to 20 s. During network training, we used simulated beads, bubbles, tubes and scanning volumes of sliced samples as the dataset and trained for 800 epochs, taking 30 h on an NVIDIA A100 GPU. Lastly, all patches were stitched together across the entire FOV using sigmoid fusion61 to generate a mesoscale, high-resolution 3D volume.Fluorescent bead imaging and resolution characterizationTo characterize the system resolution, we made a mixture of 1 μl of 1% 0.2-μm fluorescence beads (Thermo Scientific, TetraSpeck microspheres, T7280) and 1 ml of 1% agarose. The mixture was then placed in a glass-bottom dish (NEST Scientific, 801001), in which the 0.2-μm fluorescence beads were randomly distributed in the agarose and experimentally captured by RUSH3D-HR. For resolution evaluation, we quantified the resolution by measuring the FWHMs of their intensity profiles along lateral and axial dimensions. The FWHM values are presented as the mean ± s.d., which serve directly as the experimentally measured resolution values. Quantitative analysis showed that the resolution remained nearly consistent across the entire FOV, after wDAO correction for spatially nonuniform aberrations. While the resolution varied slightly at different axial planes, it remained uniform within an extended DOF of approximately 40 µm.AnimalsAll biological experiments were performed with the ethical approval from the Animal Care and Use Committee of Tsinghua University (protocol 17-DQH1.G25-1) in accordance with institutional and national guidelines.Intravital imaging in a skin injury modelMale WT C57BL/6J mice (7–8 weeks old) were housed under a 12-h light–dark cycle at 24 °C ambient temperature and 50% humidity with ad libitum access to food and water. After 4 h, a 90-μl i.v. antibody cocktail containing Alexa Fluor 647-conjugated anti-Ly6G (BioLegend, 127610), Alexa Fluor 561-conjugated anti-F4/80 (Thermo Fisher, 505-4801-82) and wheat germ agglutinin (Thermo Fisher, W11261), each at 10% dilution in PBS (Gibco, C10010500BT), was premixed before injection for neutrophil, macrophage and vascular labeling.Then, 20 min after injection, mice were anesthetized with Avertin (350 mg kg−1, i.p.), and the liver was surgically exposed on a customized imaging holder fitted with a 170-μm coverslip. Intravital imaging was performed using RUSH3D-HR system with a thermoregulator (37 °C, ThermoStar Homeothermic System, RWD) under isoflurane anesthesia (0.5% in O2 at 0.75 L min−1). Multicolor imaging was achieved using sequential 488-nm, 555-nm and 640-nm laser excitation over 50 min.Intravital imaging in an LPS-induced inflamed spleen modelSimilar to liver imaging protocols, male C57BL/6J mice (7–8 weeks old) were used for intravital spleen imaging. LPS (Escherichia coli O111:B4, Sigma-Aldrich, L2630) was prepared as a 10 mg ml−1 solution in sterile PBS (Gibco, C10010500BT) and stored at 4 °C. Mice received an i.p. injection of LPS (5 mg kg−1). Then, 30 min later, neutrophils were fluorescently labeled by i.v. injection of anti-Ly6G antibody (BioLegend, 127610) at 10% dilution in PBS through the tail vein.After a 1-h recovery period, mice were anesthetized with Avertin (350 mg kg−1, i.p.) and surgically prepared for imaging. The spleen was carefully exposed and immobilized on a customized holder with a 170-μm coverslip. Intravital imaging was performed using the RUSH3D-HR system with a thermoregulator (37 °C, ThermoStar System, RWD) under isoflurane anesthesia (0.5% in O2 at 0.75 L min−1). For data acquisition, continuous imaging was performed over 5 h with 640-nm laser excitation.EA treatmentBefore EA treatment, mice were acclimated to cylindrical restrainers for 30 min daily over three consecutive days to minimize restraint stress62,63,64. During treatment sessions, mice were placed in specialized holders that secured the trunk while allowing free movement of the head and limbs. All EA procedures were performed on conscious animals without anesthesia.The treatment was administered using two pairs of sterile stainless-steel acupuncture needles (diameter: 0.16 mm) inserted bilaterally at the ST36 (Zusanli) acupoint49. This location was precisely identified as 4 mm distal to the knee joint and 2 mm lateral to the anterior tibial tubercle. Electrical stimulation was delivered using a Master-9 stimulator (AMPI) connected to two ISO-Flex isolators (AMPI), with parameters set as follows: frequency, 10 Hz; pulse width, 50 μs; alternating current, 0–0.5 mA. Each treatment session lasted 20 min. Following EA treatment, mice underwent intravital spleen imaging as described.Mouse brain slice clearing, immunohistochemical staining and imagingMale C57BL/6J mice (8–12 weeks old) were housed under a 12-h light–dark cycle with ad libitum access to food and water. Before all perfusion experiments, mice were deeply anesthetized with Avertin working solution (0.16 ml per 10 g, containing 19.2 mg ml−1 tribromoethanol) through intraperitoneal injection. The mice were perfused with ice-cold saline followed by ice-cold 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde (PFA) in PBS. After immersing the brains in PFA for 4 h, they were vibration-sectioned into 1-mm-thick slices and rinsed with PBS for several times. The brain slices were made transparent according to the VIVIT65 procedures. For immunohistochemical staining of neuronal cell nuclei, the brain slices were incubated with primary antibodies (1:500 to 1:2,000) in PBST (0.01 M PBS containing 0.4% Triton X-100) at room temperature with shaking for 1 day. The slices were subsequently washed three times in PBST for 10 min each time. We then incubated the slices with fluorescently conjugated secondary antibodies (1:800) in PBST at room temperature with shaking for 4 h. After washing with PBS three times for 10 min each time, the slices were incubated with 2 mg ml−1 disuccinimidyl glutarate dissolved in 0.01 M PBS for 2–4 h at room temperature to prevent antibody dissociation during tissue clearing.FRC analysisThe FRC resolution66 is defined as a metric to estimate the resolution of nonstandard biological images. Firstly, high-resolution volumetric images are reconstructed using a pipeline that incorporates self-supervised denoising for noise suppression and virtual-scanning-based motion correction for artifact reduction. Secondly, to ensure the accuracy of the FRC analysis, each image is spatially partitioned into four independent subsets, I1, I2, I3 and I4, which are then transformed into the frequency domain as F1, F2, F3 and F4. We meticulously inspected these Fourier spectra to confirm the absence of isolated high-frequency peaks, which are indicators of processing artifacts, thereby ensuring the reliability of the FRC analysis. Thirdly, the FRC is subsequently computed using the following formula:$$FR{C}_{1}({r}_{i})=\frac{\sum _{r\in r{i}_{i}}{F}_{1}(r)\cdot {F}_{4}{(r)}^{\ast }}{\sqrt{\sum _{r\in {r}_{i}}{{F}_{1}}^{2}(r)\cdot \sum _{r\in {r}_{i}}{{F}_{4}}^{2}(r)}},$$$${\mathrm{FRC}}_{2}({r}_{i})=\frac{\sum _{r\in r{i}_{i}}{F}_{2}(r)\cdot {F}_{3}{(r)}^{\ast }}{\sqrt{\sum _{r\in {r}_{i}}{{F}_{2}}^{2}(r)\cdot \sum _{r\in {r}_{i}}{{F}_{3}}^{2}(r)}},$$where r represents the spatial coordinates in the frequency domain and ri is the ith frequency bin. We verified the profile of the resulting FRC curves to ensure a consistent downward trend, confirming that the correlation is driven by genuine structural information rather than correlated noise. The average of the cutoff frequencies at which FRC1(ri) and FRC2(ri) drop below the 1/7 threshold is taken as the image resolution.Performance metricsWe used lateral SBP, OTF, Strehl ratio, r.m.s. wavefront, peak-to-valley (PV) ratio, peak SNR (PSNR), structural similarity index measure (SSIM), SBR and SNR to evaluate the performance of Kunlun and RUSH3D-HR. Specifically, following the foundational principles of Fourier optics67, the lateral SBP represents the information capacity of an objective lens, defined as the product of the spatial extent and the spatial frequency bandwidth. This product effectively quantifies the total number of resolvable spots under the objective lens, which can be calculated as$$\mathrm{SBP}=4\cdot \displaystyle\frac{\pi {({d}_{\mathrm{FOV}}/2)}^{2}}{{(0.5\lambda /NA)}^{2}}$$(effective pixels) for one-photon imaging3,\({\text{SBP}}=4\cdot \displaystyle\frac{\pi {({d}_{\mathrm{FOV}}/2)}^{2}}{{(0.46\lambda /N{A}^{0.91})}^{2}}\) (effective pixels) for multiphoton imaging68, where λ is the wavelength of light and dFOV is the diameter of FOV covered by the objective lens, which can be obtained directly from the manufacturers or derived by$${d}_{{\text{FOV}}}=\frac{{\text{FN}}}{M},$$where FN and M denote the field number and magnification of objective lens, respectively.The OTF is defined as a performance metric that measures the degradation of contrast in the image compared to contrast in the object, which can be calculated as$$\mathrm{OTF}=\frac{{I}_{\max }-{I}_{\min }}{{I}_{\max }+{I}_{\min }},$$where Imax and Imin denote the maximum intensity to the white bars and minimum intensity to the black bars of a bar target at a specific frequency, respectively.The Strehl ratio is defined as a measure of the quality of optical image formation, which can be calculated as the ratio of the peak intensity of the actual PSF to the peak intensity of the ideal PSF without aberration, described as$${\text{Strehl}}\,{\text{ratio}}=\frac{{I}_{{\text{actual}}}(0)}{{I}_{{\text{ideal}}}(0)},$$where \(\mathrm{Strehl}\,\mathrm{ratio}=\frac{{I}_{\mathrm{actual}}(0)}{{I}_{\mathrm{ideal}}(0)},\) is the peak light intensity at the focal point of an actual optical system and \({I}_{\mathrm{actual}}(0)\) is the peak light intensity of an ideal diffraction-limited system, exemplified by the central intensity of an Airy disk.The r.m.s. wavefront describes the average deviation of the optical aberration from the ideal image plane, which can be calculated as$${\rm{r}}.{\rm{m}}.{\rm{s}}.=\sqrt{\frac{1}{A}{\int }_{A}{(W-\overline{W})}^{2}dA},$$where A is the image plane, W is the wavefront at the image plane and \(\overline{W}=\frac{1}{A}{\int }_{A}W\cdot dA\) is the mean wavefront deviation.The PV ratio is defined as a ratio of signal height in peak apex and signal height in the end of peak, which can be calculated as$$PV={W}_{\max }-{W}_{\min },$$where Wmax and Wmin denote the maximum value and minimum value of the wavefront, respectively.The PSNR is defined as a metric to evaluate the quality of a reconstructed or compressed signal compared to a reference signal, which can be represented as$${\text{PSNR}}=10{\mathrm{log}}_{10}\frac{max({\parallel X\parallel }_{2}^{2})}{{\parallel X-Y\parallel }_{2}^{2}}.$$The SSIM is defined as a perceptual metric that quantifies the similarity between two images by considering changes in structural information, luminance and contrast, which can be calculated as$$\mathrm{SSIM}=\frac{\left(2{\mu }_{X}{\mu }_{Y}+{(0.01\cdot \max (X))}^{2}\right)(2{\sigma }_{XY}+{(0.03\cdot \max (X))}^{2})}{\left({\mu }_{X}^{2}+{\mu }_{Y}^{2}+{(0.01\cdot \max (X))}^{2})({\sigma }_{X}^{2}+{\sigma }_{Y}^{2}+{(0.03\cdot \max (X))}^{2}\right)},$$where µX and µY are the averages of X and Y, σX and σY denote the corresponding s.d. and σXY is the corresponding cross-covariance. We firstly obtained the local SSIM maps using sliding 2D Gaussian windows (11 × 11, s.d. = 1.5) for 2D images; then, the average of these local maps was returned as the final SSIM value.The SBR is defined as the ratio of the signal intensity to the background intensity, which can be calculated as$${\text{SBR}}=10\cdot {\mathrm{log}}_{10}\frac{\max (X)}{{\mu }_{x}},$$where µX is the average of X. We firstly obtained the local SBR maps using sliding 2D Gaussian windows (200 × 200 pixels, s.d. = 30 pixels) for 2D images; then, the average of these local maps was returned as the final SBR value.The SNR is defined as a measure of signal strength relative to background noise. We used a single-image SNR estimation method69 to calculate the SNR of images without the corresponding clean images as reference, which can be represented as$$\mathrm{SNR}=\frac{{\varPhi }^{\mathrm{NF}}(0,0)-{\mu }^{2}}{\varPhi (0,0)-{\varPhi }^{\mathrm{NF}}(0,0)},$$where \(\varPhi (0,0)\) is the value of autocorrelation function (ACF) of the image at 2D offset (0, 0), µ is the average of the image and \({\varPhi }^{NF}(0,0)\) is the value of ACF of the noise-free image at zero offset, which can be estimated with the average of \(\varPhi (0,1)\) and \(\varPhi (0,-1)\).Data analysisThe data acquisition was accomplished using our customized image acquisition software. All data processing and analysis were accomplished with customized Python (version 3.8.20) and MATLAB (MathWorks, MATLAB 2019a) scripts. The 3D rendering was performed by Imaris (version 9.0.1) with ‘volume’ modules. The 3D tracking of neutrophils in living mouse dorsal skins and spleens was carried out using Imaris (version 9.0.1) with the ‘cell tracking’ function. The 3D cell pose segmentation of neutrophils in mouse skins was carried out using the Cellpose70 algorithm. For migrasome event detection in living mouse livers, we detected all contours on neutrophil channel each frame and filtered contours with diameter < 3 μm, which is approximately the maximum diameter of a migrasome71. The circularity of each contour was calculated using \(\frac{4\pi A}{{C}^{2}}\), where A represents the size of the contour and C represents the circumference of the contour. The contours with circularity < 0.4 were filtered out. To exclude contours caused by noise, we further calculated the local intensity range by subtracting from the local minimum intensity from center intensity and the local intensity range less than half of the whole frame intensity range was filtered out. Lastly, the contours that appeared at least twice in the following three frames were considered as migrasome contours. Once a migrasome contour was detected, its center intensity and intensity at the same location on previous frame were recorded. In the following frames, the position deviation of this contour was recorded for speed calculation.Reporting summaryFurther information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.
